1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is deterministic and machine-verifiable: each terminal puzzle (Sys32/BitMixer/Cipher/Hash/Maze) has a server-side correctness check (status['done']/online/total) plus per-terminal unit_tests.py and trace_test.py. The agent's success is judged by whether it actually solves the terminal, not by string matching.

2_construct_validity.justification: Headline eval (run_adaptive_learning -> adaptive_learning) runs a genuine agentic tool loop: run_agent_loop calls the model via _run_genai_tool_loop/_run_openai_compatible_manual_tool_loop against a live terminal server, scoring cross-run learning by how many progressively harder generators it solves. The progressive T1..T5 generator ladder isolates 'adaptive learning / progress retention' specifically.

3_code_quality.justification: Substantial, modular infrastructure (server/, terminals/, VFS, telemetry, checkpoint save/restore, free-port server, deterministic seeding rnd=Random(42)) with per-terminal unit tests. The notebook's import error (kaggle_benchmarks.envs.LocalEnvironment missing) is a version-pin/environmental artifact, not a construction defect; the imported package logic is clean and well-organized.

4_sample_size.justification: The eval set is the 10 task generators (T1, T1-T1, ... composite). As distinct evaluation items this is well under 20; the benchmark is a single adaptive ladder rather than a large item bank, so sample size for statistical comparison is low (1-9 effective tiers).

5_metric_design.justification: The score combines number of fully-passed generators with an iterations-spent 'fine' (score = 100*passed - min(99, iters/passed)), blending capability and efficiency into one scalar. Telemetry tracks iterations, but the reported metric is a single composite leaderboard number with no second independent dimension reported.

6_problem_statement.justification: The @task description states the target ('capacity for cross-run learning and progress retention by presenting increasingly complex versions of a task'), naming the capability and method, but the notebook markdown is thin and does not situate the gap relative to existing agentic benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: The dataset package is the documentation: docstrings, per-terminal .md specs, and structured code. But the notebook itself has only brief markdown (install/import/run), and there is no consolidated description of scoring rules/schema for a reader, requiring code reading.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (incremental skill retention under escalating difficulty) with a built-in difficulty gradient (T1 single terminal -> multi-terminal composites) acting as an experimental control; success at higher tiers attributable to learning/transfer rather than one-shot luck.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are an explicit difficulty taxonomy: a 10-step generator ladder mixing terminal types (Sys32/BitMixer/Cipher/Hash/Maze) and composition depth, with the harness reporting which generator/task the model failed on (_find_easiest_unsolved_task emits failed_on_generator/failed_on_task), enabling a where-it-fails gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: No run.json artifact or confirmed model run is present (the notebook errored at import due to a version-pin mismatch, an environmental issue). No evidence of more than one model; deterministic count is 1.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Hybrid scoring: deterministic word-count and forbidden-token (lexical guillotine) checks are unambiguous, but the substantive judgment uses a 4-judge LLM senate with criteria phrased as yes/no questions (3-4 dimensions). The rubric questions lack anchored examples/score scales, so the LLM-judge component is a vague-ish multi-dimension rubric.

2_construct_validity.justification: Each notebook genuinely calls the target model (raw=llm.prompt(prompt)), extracts the spoken block, then scores it. The design deliberately isolates 'epistemic exile / concept-mapping under constraint' and includes paired conditions (English baseline vs Ge'ez / Spanish native-language; same content) to control for surface difficulty, tightly matching the stated purpose.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean and consistent across the 5 notebooks: regex extraction with fallback on missing tags, deterministic word-count normalization, per-token lexical regex, structured senate vote ledger with dissent logging, and explicit guards (senate-size check). Uses @kbench.task and assess_response_with_judge idiomatically.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook evaluates exactly ONE prompt/scenario (a single llm.prompt call); the benchmark is 5 separate single-item tasks. Per-task item count is 1 and the aggregate is ~5 hand-crafted scenarios -- far below the 10-item threshold, so a single response swings each task's pass/fail.

5_metric_design.justification: Multiple distinct dimensions are computed: deterministic structural compliance (word count), deterministic lexical purity (forbidden-token absence), and semantic/conceptual judgment via a 4-judge consensus (75% threshold), and multi-judge voting mitigates single-judge bias -- spanning format, constraint-adherence, and content correctness.

6_problem_statement.justification: The task descriptions clearly name the capability ('Evaluating Epistemic Exile', metacognition, concept mapping under chronological constraint) and give vivid motivation, but do not connect to a research gap or existing benchmarks; framing is largely flavor text.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Docstrings and inline section banners explain the extraction/word-count/lexical/senate stages, but there is no consolidated task-page-level write-up of scoring thresholds, judge selection rationale, or schema; documentation is partial and embedded in code comments.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a specific sub-capability (mapping modern concepts into a period/linguistic frame without anachronistic vocabulary) with an explicit experimental control: paired English-baseline vs native-language variants of the same scenario isolate the linguistic-constraint effect from the conceptual-mapping ability.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items form a structured set of paired conditions across scenarios (AGI/Aksum in Ge'ez vs English; social-media/Bolivar in Spanish vs English; Stuxnet/Taytu) with per-criterion senate breakdown (Linguistic_Purity, metaphor, Chronological_Purity), enabling analysis of which dimension fails and language-vs-baseline gradients.

10_model_coverage.justification: No run.json artifact captured a target model; outputs are stripped and only one chat history is shown. No evidence the suite was run against multiple distinct target models; deterministic count is effectively 1.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Every World-Swap problem has a deterministically generated correct and naive answer (apply_operator_swap, apply_ling, apply_logic, PHYSICS_RULES). Verification normalizes and uses multi-layer matching (verbose-correct guard, substring, numeric proximity, embedding/token-overlap) with structured output (UnifiedResp), making correctness fully verifiable from code.

2_construct_validity.justification: Headline aggregate() genuinely runs 600 llm.prompt(...) trials with structured schema and scores the model's actual answers/confidence. Construct (metacognitive efficiency) is isolated via SDT meta-d'/d', and a deliberate confound control: TIER1_PROMPT is scaffold-equalized with UNIFIED_PROMPT (identical structure, differing only in world rules) to remove scaffolding as a confound for tier comparisons.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular, heavily sectioned: deterministic seeds, dataclasses, parallel execution with per-row _safe_call exception handling, protobuf sanitization, NaN/inf guards in SDT math, fallback embedding->token-overlap slop detection, and tool-execution cost logic. Robust output parsing and graceful degradation throughout.

4_sample_size.justification: N=600 generated items (150/tier x 4 tiers, balanced across 4 problem types), far exceeding 20, with assertions requiring >=500 valid trials for stable SDT estimates and bootstrap CIs (5000 resamples).

5_metric_design.justification: Many genuinely distinct dimensions: SDT d' and meta-d'/M-ratio, ACE (asymmetric Brier), Selective-Prediction score, Tool-Utility, ECE, confidence entropy, four failure-mode rates, plus bootstrap 95% CIs -- spanning discrimination, calibration, decision quality, and tool use, with justification in markdown.

6_problem_statement.justification: The header and task description name the capability (metacognition, meta-d'/d' efficiency, four failure modes) and method clearly, but the framing is a notebook header rather than a task page that situates the gap versus prior metacognition benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Comprehensive: sectioned markdown for World-Swap engine, slop detection, dataset generator (N=600 schema), evaluation (meta-d'+ACE+selective prediction), prompts, and aggregator; metric definitions and the scaffold-equalization design rationale are explained in code/markdown, sufficient to reproduce.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (metacognitive monitoring/calibration under counterfactual world-swaps) with explicit controls: identity-rule Tier 1 baseline vs swapped Tier 2-4, congruent scaffolding, inverse-scaling index, and a stated hypothesis tied to meta-d'/d' and prior-leakage (semantic slop).

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are an explicit 4-tier x 4-type taxonomy; the profile reports per-tier accuracy/confidence (Tier1Acc..Tier4Acc), inverse-scaling index, per-failure-mode rates, and calibration bins -- a rich where-models-fail gradient, not just an aggregate.

10_model_coverage.justification: Only one run.json present (adversarial..._Run_1_anthropic_claude-opus-4-7default). Single model; deterministic count is 1.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Questions carry ground-truth answers and the LLM-calling notebooks score against them, but verification is fragile: score_answer/_answer_matches use lowercased substring and >0.7 token-overlap (no normalization of edge cases), and staleness uses loose keyword matching ('stale'/'old'/'outdated'). Ground truth exists but matching is brittle.

2_construct_validity.justification: The headline temporalbench-eval and the captured library task (tasks/as_of.py) genuinely call llm.prompt and score model answers vs ground truth, so the suite does measure a model -- but construct validity is moderately confounded: substring/overlap scoring conflates correctness with formatting, and two prominent notebooks (temporalbench-v2-pccc, temporalbenchv4) never use `llm`, instead asserting store.get(...) is not None and incrementing correct unconditionally (942/942, 734/734 by construction), with temporalbenchv4 even %choose-ing a self-asserting PastQueryTrap for the leaderboard.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and produces results, but rough: tasks swallow all per-item exceptions with bare 'except Exception: pass', the v2/v4 notebooks contain dead `llm` parameters and tautological correct+=1 loops, and large blocks of fact-parsing logic are duplicated verbatim across notebooks rather than shared.

4_sample_size.justification: Datasets are large (e.g. 2000 questions, 5480 facts per version; v4 OverlapQuery alone has 734 items) across v1-v4 and multiple seeds -- far exceeding 20 evaluation items.

5_metric_design.justification: Each task computes a single correct/total accuracy with no second independent dimension and no bias mitigation; the saved results 'key_findings' (TRS/ablation/correlation numbers) are narrative strings not computed in the notebook, so effectively one metric (accuracy) is produced.

6_problem_statement.justification: Headers name the capability (temporal reasoning, validity windows vs decay, task families AsOfQA/PastQueryTrap/etc.) and motivate via 'validity windows beat decay', but do not concretely position the gap against existing temporal-reasoning benchmarks beyond slogans.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Dataset provenance (Kaggle dataset id, file structure, question keys) and task families are described, and there is some inline explanation, but scoring rules/edge cases are under-documented and the inconsistency between LLM-scored and self-asserting notebooks is never reconciled for a reader.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets the temporal-reasoning domain with thematically distinct task families (PastQueryTrap, DecayTrap, OverlapQuery) approximating difficulty tiers v1->v4, but lacks rigorous experimental controls in the model-facing eval (the adversarial 'traps' that would isolate the mechanism live mainly in the self-asserting store notebooks, not the LLM-scored ones).

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly organized by task_family and by version tier (v1 baseline -> v4 adversarial), and the per-notebook tasks filter and report results per task_family, giving a structured taxonomy and a where-models-fail breakdown across families/difficulty.

10_model_coverage.justification: Across the run.json artifacts the suite was run on 3 distinct models: zai/glm-5 (temporalbench-eval), google/gemini-2.5-flash (temporal-ambiguity), and anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 (staleness, v4) -- 2-3 distinct models.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Fully verifiable, deterministic ground truth in all three sub-tasks: regex-golf uses re.fullmatch against MATCH/DO_NOT_MATCH lists (pre-validated baselines); code-golf execs candidate code against hidden assert-based unit tests; EGAT scores via exact geometric distance to a fixed centroid. No subjective judgment.

2_construct_validity.justification: All three notebooks run genuine multi-turn LLM loops (llm.prompt in a 10-trial feedback loop) and score the model's actual outputs. Each isolates a specific constraint-reasoning capability (regex compression, code compression, embedding-space navigation) with a fractional-reduction metric directly tied to the claim; the feedback loop measures adaptation under constraint as stated.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, robust, well-commented: regex extraction with bare-fence fallback, fresh-namespace exec with traceback-limited error feedback, system-role-rejection detection with baked-prompt restart, consecutive-API-error backoff/abort, deterministic order-preserving dedup, whitespace-token filtering for tokenizer parity, and per-session chat contexts. Strong edge-case handling.

4_sample_size.justification: Each sub-task has only 5 sessions/tasks/prompts (5 regex sessions, 5 code tasks, 5 EGAT prompts). Even pooled per notebook this is 5 evaluation items -- below the 10-item threshold, so individual session variance dominates the mean score.

5_metric_design.justification: A single primary dimension is reported per notebook (mean fractional reduction / fractional improvement), with a -1.0 no-solution penalty. The metric is well-justified in markdown and appropriate, but there is essentially one performance dimension (compression/convergence ratio) rather than two distinct dimensions.

6_problem_statement.justification: Each notebook's markdown clearly names the capability (pattern compression / syntactical efficiency / embedding-geometry awareness) and explains the scoring, but motivation stays at the task level and does not articulate the broader research gap versus existing coding/regex benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Thorough across notebooks: markdown tables of sessions/baselines, explicit scoring formulas with sign interpretation, configuration-flag tables (EGAT), pre-validation notes for baselines, and detailed inline comments explaining the reprompt protocol, tokenizer-parity fix, and system-role fallback -- enough to reproduce.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Each probes a specific, well-defined sub-capability with a clear hypothesis and a difficulty gradient as control (regex sessions ordered by baseline complexity 21->56 chars; code tasks 449->1465 chars; EGAT prompts 1->19 tokens of increasing adversariality), letting performance be attributed to constraint-adaptation skill.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are an explicit difficulty taxonomy with titled, stratified sessions/tasks (each labeled and ordered by baseline length / token count) and per-session scores reported individually (Per-session/Per-task/Per-prompt arrays), revealing where models succeed vs fail along the gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: No run.json artifact names a target model and only a single live run is shown per notebook (EGAT executed once); no evidence of multiple distinct models, so deterministic count is 1.
